nativeRange.habitat: Standing or slow-moving freshwater — ponds, oxbow lakes, ditches, marshes, and the quiet margins of slow rivers; floats freely on the surface where there is full or partial sun and humid air

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface for floating mats
low to moderate surface agitation — strong flow or spray bars break up the velvety leaf coating
open-top or rim-only lid to allow leaves to stay above the waterline

goodWith: live-bearing fish fry (provides shade and shelter)
labyrinth fish (gouramis, bettas) that use surface cover for bubble-nesting
avoidWith: tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or persistent overhead drip that wets the leaves

summary: Common Salvinia (Salvinia natans) is a small floating aquatic fern with paired nickel-sized leaves coated in water-repellent hairs. Forms a fast-spreading surface mat that shades the tank, helps suppress algae, and gives fry and shrimp cover.
careNotes: Salvinia natans is one of a handful of fully aquatic ferns kept in the aquarium hobby. Each plant carries two velvety, kidney-shaped floating leaves and a third submerged, finely-divided leaf that hangs below and functions as a root analog — the genus has no true roots, and all nutrient uptake happens through the leaf surfaces. The upper leaves are covered in tiny eggbeater-shaped trichomes that trap a thin layer of air, making the leaves strongly hydrophobic; persistently wet leaves (from spray bars, surface drip, or splashing filter returns) will rot at the base. Several other Salvinia species are sold under similar trade names — most commonly S. minima (the Tropica catalog plant), S. molesta (the giant invasive 'kariba weed'), S. cucullata (cupped leaves) and S. auriculata — so verify the species before purchase; S. natans specifically has flat paired leaves rather than the folded-cup shape of S. cucullata or the deeply-arched 'chains' of mature S. molesta. Husbandry is undemanding: low to moderate light is enough, no CO2 needed, fertilization can stay light, and temperature 18-28 °C covers the typical community-tank window. Sources lean warm-water (Bantam.earth and aqualandpetsplus both list ~20-30 °C as optimal), but S. natans is notably cold-tolerant for a Salviniaceae and can be summered outdoors in temperate ponds across Europe and North America; it tends to die back as an annual through winter rather than overwintering as live plant, regenerating from spores or stored fragments next season. Growth rate is fast — fishkeepingadventure.com cites biomass doubling in days under good conditions — so plan on regular thinning to keep the mat from sealing the surface and starving submerged plants of light. Cross-source disagreement is minor: pH range varies from 5.0-8.0 (growplants.org) through 6.0-7.5 (fishkeepingadventure) to 6.5-7.5 (Buce Plant, Bantam); 6.0-7.5 used as the practical aquarium-keeping window. Note that the Tropica plantdetails URL initially supplied for this species actually maps to S. minima in Tropica's catalog — Tropica does not appear to list S. natans separately — so Buce Plant was promoted to primary and Bantam.earth + fishkeepingadventure.com + aqualandpetsplus.com used as secondaries.
breedingNotes: Propagates almost exclusively by vegetative fragmentation in aquaria — break a healthy mat into smaller pieces and the daughter plantlets continue from the lateral nodes. Spore production via sporocarps does occur under outdoor pond conditions with seasonal temperature swings, and is the route by which S. natans overwinters in temperate climates, but spore-based propagation is not practical in tropical aquaria. Plant is heterosporous (separate micro- and megasporocarps), like other ferns in Salviniaceae.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the supplied Tropica plantdetails URL (/4767/4767) actually returns Salvinia minima, not S. natans — Tropica does not list S. natans in its catalog. Buce Plant page is product-specific to S. natans and provides pH 6.5-7.5, light low to high, CO2 not necessary, growth rate fast, size 2-3 inches, propagation by side shoots, placement floating, care difficulty easy.

additional.name: Bantam.earth
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Salviniaceae), native range (central Europe), pH (6.5-7.5), placement (surface; roots submerged, leaves in open air), propagation (division and spores on shoot tips), and leaf size (~0.5-1 inch). Lists temperature 60-70 °F (15-21 °C) — cooler than other sources, reflecting temperate vivarium use.

additional.name: Fishkeepingadventure.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20-28 °C with tolerance to 15 °C in temperate zones), pH (6.0-7.5), growth rate (extremely rapid, biomass doubling possible in days), native range (central/eastern Europe, Asia, parts of Africa), propagation (spore reproduction plus vegetative fragmentation), and cold tolerance caveat.

additional.name: Aqualandpetsplus.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (20-30 °C optimal), pH (6.5-7.5), gH (4-15 dGH) and kH (3-8 dKH) — the only source giving hardness ranges. Confirms fast growth via stolons and limited tolerance to drops below 20 °C without dormancy.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Salvinia natans
additional.notes: Taxonomy reference: family Salviniaceae, order Salviniales, division Polypodiophyta. Confirms broad Eurasian + North African native distribution (Algeria, Egypt, Morocco, France, Germany, Ukraine, China, Japan, Taiwan, etc.), IUCN Least Concern, and naturalized/introduced status in parts of the northeastern United States.

propagation: sporocarp / spore production (mainly under outdoor pond conditions)
